Only take nitrofurantoin if a healthcare provider confirms or strongly suspects a urinary tract infection. Avoid using it for viral infections or other conditions where it won't help, to prevent antibiotic resistance and side effects.

Always finish the full prescribed course of nitrofurantoin, even if you start feeling better. Stopping early can allow bacteria to survive, potentially causing the infection to come back and increasing the chance of resistance.

Keep track of any unusual symptoms such as breathing difficulties, chest pain, cough, or numbness and tingling in the hands or feet. Report these promptly to a healthcare provider to prevent serious complicaions.

Do not share your nitrofurantoin with other people, even if they have similar symptoms. Using someone else's medicine can be unsafe and may contribute to ineffective treatment or antibiotic resistance.

Store nitrofurantoin in a cool, dry place at room temperature, away from direct sunlight, moisture, or heat. Proper storage helps maintain the effectiveness and safety of the medication throughout its shelf life.

Nitrofurantoin should only be used to treat urinary tract infections caused by bacteria that are sensitive to the medicine. It should not be used for viral infections or infections outside the urinary tract. Using the medicine only when needed helps prevent bacteria from becoming resistant and keeps the drug effective in the future.

The medicine should be taken exactly as prescribed by the doctor. Adults should take 50–100 mg every 6 hours with meals, and children should take the prescribed dose based on body weight. Taking it with food helps improve absorption and reduces stomach upset. Do not skip doses and complete the full course even if symptoms improve early.

During prolonged therapy, especially in elderly patients, liver and lung function should be checked regularly. Patients with existing lung, liver, nerve, or kidney problems should be monitored closely. If symptoms such as breathing difficulty, numbness, tingling, or weakness occur, the medicine should be stopped and medical advice sought immediately.

To reduce the risk of side effects, avoid use in patients with contraindications such as anuria, oliguria, hepatitis, late pregnancy, or infants under 1 month old. Careful patient selection helps prevent serious complications.

Store Nitrofurantoin at temperatures not exceeding 30°C. Keep it in a dry place away from direct heat and moisture. Proper storage ensures that the medicine remains stable and effective until its expiration date.
